title: Foundation (Makeup) on Silk
stainChemistry: Classification: Combination (oil + mineral pigment). Foundation is an oil-in-water or water-in-oil emulsion containing: (1) OIL PHASE — dimethicone (silicone), mineral oil, or fatty acid esters as the primary carrier, (2) MINERAL PIGMENT — iron oxides (Fe₂O₃ for warm tones), titanium dioxide (TiO₂ for coverage/lightening), zinc oxide for mattifying, (3) EMULSIFIERS + WATER — hold the oil and water phases together, (4) sometimes TALC — magnesium silicate for texture. This is primarily an oil stain with suspended mineral particles. POG dissolves the oil carrier, releasing the mineral pigment for extraction. The pigment is inorganic — it doesn't dissolve in solvents and doesn't respond to bleach. On silk, the oil penetrates the protein fiber quickly, and mineral particles can become trapped in the fiber's natural texture.
whyThisWorks: POG dissolves the oil/silicone carrier that binds the mineral pigment to the fiber. Once the oil is dissolved, the mineral particles (iron oxide, titanium dioxide) are freed and can be flushed out mechanically. NSD then addresses any remaining water-soluble emulsifier residue and protein-based ingredients (some foundations contain collagen or silk amino acids). On silk, staying dryside as long as possible prevents water-spotting while the POG does its work on the oil phase.

spottingProtocol.technique: Controlled blotting to contain the stain
spottingProtocol.instruction: Gently blot surface foundation with a clean white pad. Press and lift — never rub or wipe, which smears the oil and pigment across a larger area. On silk, even blotting must be gentle — the fiber is fragile. Remove as much surface material as possible before introducing chemistry. If the foundation is thick (full-coverage formula), you can gently scrape excess with a credit card edge, but be careful not to scratch the silk.
spottingProtocol.equipment: Clean white cotton pads, credit card (optional)

spottingProtocol.agent: A.L. Wilson NovaBone POG or equivalent
spottingProtocol.technique: Dissolve the oil carrier — primary treatment, dryside on silk
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply POG agent to the foundation stain. Place garment face-down on spotting board over clean towel. Apply from the back to push dissolved foundation out. The POG dissolves the silicone/oil carrier, releasing the suspended mineral pigment. Light tapping only on silk — this fiber is weak when wet with solvent. You should see tan/beige color transferring to the towel underneath. Flush with VDS. Repeat 2-3 times until color transfer significantly decreases. On silk, the oil may have penetrated deeper than visible — treat an area slightly larger than the visible stain.
spottingProtocol.equipment: POG agent, spotting board, spotting brush (light pressure), VDS, clean white towels

spottingProtocol.agent: NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) — gentle wetside treatment
spottingProtocol.technique: Remove water-soluble residue and emulsifier components
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply NSD to address the water-soluble emulsifier phase and any remaining oil residue that didn't fully flush with VDS. On silk, apply neutral lubricant first, then NSD with gentle water flush from the water gun at safe distance (6+ inches per Eisen). Work gently — silk is weakest in the wetside phase. Flush until the pad shows no more color transfer. The NSD also removes any protein-based ingredients (collagen, keratin) found in some foundations.
spottingProtocol.equipment: Neutral lubricant, NSD, water gun, clean pads

spottingProtocol.agent: Acetic acid for mineral pigment residue
spottingProtocol.technique: Gentle acid treatment for iron oxide particles on silk
spottingProtocol.instruction: If a warm-toned color shadow remains after POG + NSD treatment, it's likely iron oxide pigment embedded in the silk. Apply dilute acetic acid (28% GAA at 1:10) — this mildly chelates iron without the aggressiveness of oxalic acid or HF. On silk, acetic acid is one of the few safe acids (silk pH tolerance: 4.0-7.0). Work briefly, flush immediately with water. Do NOT use hydrogen peroxide for iron oxide on silk — peroxide can weaken silk protein, and it's not very effective on mineral pigments anyway. If the shadow is white/chalky, it's titanium dioxide — this is very difficult to remove and may require extended mechanical flushing.

spottingProtocol.agent: Leveling agent — prevent water rings on silk
spottingProtocol.instruction: Mandatory on silk. Spray leveling agent onto the entire treated area and extend 2-3 inches beyond the wet boundary. Feather the edges. On silk, water ring prevention is as important as the stain removal itself.

Air dry completely. Inspect under strong angled light — foundation stains are often skin-toned and can blend with fabric, so check at multiple angles. On silk, any residual mineral pigment or ring mark will be visible in the natural sheen. If shadow remains, repeat from step 2. Once clean, press with low heat only.

homeSolutions: Blot excess foundation immediately — press with a clean white cloth, lift, rotate to clean area. Never wipe or rub.
Apply a small amount of micellar water (makeup remover) to a cotton pad. Gently press onto the stain — micellar water is designed to dissolve foundation formulas. Blot, don't rub.
Follow with a drop of Dawn dish soap mixed with cool water. Dab gently onto silk — NEVER rub. The surfactant emulsifies the remaining oil phase.

Rinse by dabbing with a pad dampened in cool water + a splash of white vinegar. Air dry flat. Take to a dry cleaner if any color remains — aggressive treatment on silk at home risks more damage than the stain.
materialWarnings: Silk is a PROTEIN fiber — NEVER use protease enzymes.
NEVER use chlorine bleach on silk.
Silk water-spots instantly — leveling agent is mandatory.
Keep pH 4.0-7.0 on silk. Ammonia weakens fibroin.
Hydrogen peroxide above 3% weakens silk — and it's ineffective on mineral pigments anyway.
Mineral pigment (iron oxide, TiO₂) doesn't dissolve in solvents — it must be mechanically extracted.
Some foundations contain SPF ingredients (avobenzone, zinc oxide) that create additional mineral deposits requiring separate treatment.
Silicon-based foundations (dimethicone) may leave a water-repellent film on silk that requires additional VDS flushing.
escalation: If mineral pigment persists: gentle oxalic acid application is possible but rated D (limited use) on silk — professional use only, very brief contact, immediate neutralization. For titanium dioxide (white residue), there is no good chemical solution — extended mechanical flushing is the best approach. On silk, know when to stop: aggressive chemical escalation risks fiber damage worse than a faint foundation shadow. For extreme cases, a textile conservation specialist may be needed.

professional.name: A.L. Wilson NovaBone POG Remover
professional.amazonSearchQuery: AL Wilson NovaBone POG remover professional spotting
professional.note: Dissolves the oil/silicone carrier in foundation formulas.

professional.name: NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent)
professional.note: Addresses emulsifier residue and water-soluble foundation components.

professional.name: Sofspot Leveling Agent
professional.note: Prevents water rings on silk — mandatory after wet treatment.

household.name: Micellar Water (Makeup Remover)
household.note: Designed to dissolve foundation — micelles encapsulate oil and pigment for removal.

household.name: Dawn Dish Soap (Original Blue)
household.note: Oil-cutting surfactant. Use sparingly on silk with cool water.

customerExplanation: Foundation is an oil-based formula with mineral pigment particles (iron oxide gives it color, titanium dioxide provides coverage). We dissolve the oil carrier with professional solvents, then flush the freed mineral particles out of the fabric. On silk, we take extra care to prevent water marks by using professional leveling agents — the treatment itself can leave marks if not properly managed.
commonMistakes: Wiping or rubbing foundation — this spreads the oil and embeds pigment deeper. Always blot. | Using water before dissolving the oil — water won't touch an oil-based foundation. Start with POG or oil-based remover. | Trying to bleach mineral pigment — iron oxide and titanium dioxide don't respond to oxidizing or reducing bleach. They're minerals, not dyes. | Skipping the leveling agent on silk.
scienceNote: Foundation is classified as a combination stain per RR Street: oil (dry-side) + water-soluble emulsifiers (wet-side). The mineral pigments (iron oxides, TiO₂) are inert particles that resist all chemical treatment — they can only be physically extracted. This makes foundation more of a mechanical problem than a chemical one. The POG dissolves the glue (oil) holding the particles to the fiber; the vacuum provides the extraction force. Home methods struggle because they lack the vacuum extraction — they dissolve the oil but can't fully remove the freed mineral particles.
callPro: Foundation on silk should go to a professional dry cleaner. The combination of oil-based carrier, mineral pigment, and silk's water-spotting tendency requires spotting board technique with vacuum extraction and leveling agents that home methods can't replicate.
difficulty_factors: Silk is delicate — water-spots, weak when wet, limited chemical tolerance
Mineral pigment doesn't dissolve — must be mechanically extracted
Oil penetrates silk protein fiber quickly — treat promptly
solventNote: For PERC shops: PERC on silk requires caution. GreenEarth or hydrocarbon preferred. Pre-spot foundation on board — machine processing alone may leave mineral pigment residue. Level with Sofspot. For wet clean: silk wet cleaning is high-risk.
